claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 13 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 15 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 13 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 15 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 13 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 15 (under 16); Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £26.05/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child in care for 4 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**Could you please tell me:**
1. How old is your child? (If you have multiple children, let's start with one and work through each)
2. What is your child's name?

This will help me determine which eligibility rules apply to your situation.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hi! Yes, I have two children I'd like to ask about.

My first is Alex, who's 13 years old. And then I also have Blake, who's 15.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Thank you for that information. Let me assess eligibility for both Alex (13) and Blake (15).

Since both children are under 16, they fall into the automatic qualification age category. Let me check the next requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news so far! Now I need to check another requirement:

**Are both Alex (13) and Blake (15) working less than 24 hours per week?** (Or are they not in paid work at all?)

This is a standard requirement to ensure children aren't working full-time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. They're both still in school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they meet this requirement. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are either Alex or Blake receiving any benefits in their own right?** For example:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or similar benefits claimed in their own name

Since they're 13 and 15 and still in school, I expect the answer is no, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next check:

**Are either Alex or Blake enrolled in any type of apprenticeship?**

This matters because certain apprenticeships can affect eligibility, depending on the type and region (England, Scotland, or Wales).

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are doing apprenticeships. They're just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! They're in regular school, so this doesn't apply to them. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about **your residency status** (as the claimant):

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

If yes, that's the most straightforward path. If your situation is different (for example, if you have pre-settled status, are a Crown servant abroad, or have another special circumstance), please let me know and I can assess those specific paths.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now an important question about your relationship with the children:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?** 

This is satisfied if ANY of the following apply:
- You live with them, OR
- You contribute to their upkeep (paying at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care - things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing), OR
- You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me. But Blake doesn't live with me anymore - they live with their mum. But I do help out financially for Blake - I contribute towards things like clothes and school expenses.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. This is helpful information.

**For Alex:** You live together, so you clearly meet the responsibility requirement.

**For Blake:** You're contributing financially towards clothes and school expenses. To meet the responsibility requirement for Blake, you need to be contributing at least the equivalent amount of Child Benefit towards their upkeep.

Let me check the current Child Benefit rates for you:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
The exact current rates aren't specified in my system, but generally Child Benefit is around £25 per week for the eldest child and about £16-17 per week for additional children. 

**So for Blake, the question is:** Are you contributing at least around £16-17 per week (or roughly £70+ per month) towards Blake's upkeep through things like clothes, school expenses, and other support?

If yes, then you meet the responsibility requirement for Blake as well.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I contribute £26.05 per week towards Blake's expenses.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! That's a specific amount that clearly meets the contribution requirement for Blake. So you meet the responsibility requirement for both children:
- **Alex:** Living with you
- **Blake:** You're contributing £26.05 per week towards their upkeep

Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Important question now:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for either Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Is Blake's mum (who Blake lives with) claiming Child Benefit for Blake?
- Is anyone claiming for Alex?

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No one else is claiming for Alex. But for Blake - yes, their mum is already claiming Child Benefit for them since Blake lives with her.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that important information. This creates different situations for each child:

**For Alex:** No one else is claiming, so we can continue checking your eligibility.

**For Blake:** Since Blake's mum is already claiming Child Benefit for them, you cannot also claim Child Benefit for Blake. Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. 

The person who the child lives with typically has priority for the claim. Since Blake lives with their mum and she's already claiming, she would be the eligible claimant for Blake.

However, if Blake's mum agreed to stop claiming and transfer the claim to you, that would be different. But based on what you've told me, it sounds like she is actively claiming and Blake lives with her.

**So let me continue the assessment for Alex only.** Let me check the next requirement for Alex:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now for Alex specifically:

**Which of these situations applies to Alex?**
- Living normally at home with you
- Currently in hospital
- In local authority care
- Fostered

I assume Alex is living normally at home with you, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Let me continue with the next check for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Final question for Alex:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council providing financial support specifically for Alex's living costs or care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me navigate to the final outcome for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Now let me provide you with the complete assessment and report the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## **Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment - Complete**

I've completed the assessment for both of your children. Here's the outcome:

---

### **For Alex (age 13): ✅ ELIGIBLE**

**Good news!** You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. Alex meets all the eligibility requirements:
- Under 16 years old (automatic qualification)
- Not working full-time
- Not receiving benefits in their own right
- Not in an apprenticeship
- You meet UK residency requirements
- You have responsibility for Alex (living together)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- Alex lives normally at home with you
- The local council isn't paying towards Alex's accommodation

---

### **For Blake (age 15): ❌ NOT ELIGIBLE (for your claim)**

Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because **Blake's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for them**. 

Under UK rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. Even though you contribute £26.05 per week towards Blake's upkeep (which would normally satisfy the responsibility requirement), the fact that Blake's mother has an active claim means you cannot also claim.

---

### **Next Steps for Alex:**

1. **Apply for Child Benefit** - You can apply online or by post using form CH2
2. **Documents needed:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Alex's birth certificate or adoption certificate
3. **Important:** If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, the High Income Child Benefit Charge may apply (this is a tax charge, not an eligibility issue)
4. **Payments:** Child Benefit is typically paid every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

Do you have any questions about this assessment or the application process?

reasonings: Alex (age 13) meets all eligibility requirements: (1) Under 16 years old - automatic age qualification; (2) Not working 24+ hours per week; (3) Not receiving benefits in their own right; (4) Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Claimant meets UK residency requirements (normally lives in Manchester); (6) Claimant has responsibility for Alex (lives together); (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally at home; (9) Local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance. Therefore, the claimant is ELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake (age 15) does not qualify for this claimant's Child Benefit claim because Blake's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake. Under UK Child Benefit rules, only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. While the claimant meets the responsibility requirement by contributing £26.05 per week towards Blake's upkeep, and Blake meets all the child-related criteria (under 16, not working, not receiving benefits, not in apprenticeship), the existence of another active claimant (Blake's mother, with whom Blake lives) prevents this claimant from also claiming for Blake. Therefore, the claimant is INELIGIBLE to claim Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (13) and Blake (15). The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex, who lives with them and meets all eligibility criteria with no other claimant. However, the claimant cannot claim for Blake because Blake's mother is already claiming Child Benefit for Blake, and only one person can claim per child at a time.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child
